transcript: What is an index? Presented by WallStreetSurvivor.com An index is a quick and easy way to get an idea about how a general market is performing. Here's Jessica. Jessica is in high school and is running for class president. Before the election, Jessica wants to see how she stands to find out what her chances are of winning. The problem is her class is 500 people, and asking everyone will be too much work. So what's the alternative? Instead, Jessica decides to poll a sample of students to at least get an idea of the percentage of students that would vote for her. It's not a perfect solution, but it's much easier. Similarly, an index is a sample of stocks that provide a glimpse at the broader market. When you hear someone say the market is up 100 points, the market they're referring to is an index. There are three major US indices, the Dow Jones Industrial Average, the S&P 500, and the NASDAQ Composite Index. The Dow is the oldest and most famous index. It takes a weighted average of the 30 largest stocks on the New York Stock Exchange. These 30 stocks are so heavily traded that they represent the majority of the market volume. Therefore, these 30 stocks are generally a good indicator of the entire market. The S&P index takes a weighted average of the 500 most widely held stocks on the market. These 500 stocks account for approximately 70% of the entire market. Most professionals use the S&P's performance as their benchmark. Lastly, the NASDAQ looks at over 3,000 stocks. The NASDAQ used to be an index exclusively for tech stocks, but has grown to include a few stocks from different industries as well. You can learn more about indices by checking out wallstreetsurvivor.com.
